title: Preference Datasets
lesson:
A preference dataset teaches a model not just an answer, but which of two answers people prefer. Each row is a tiny contest: one prompt, two responses, and a label saying which one won. That is the whole idea.

Where an SFT dataset shows the model a single ideal answer to copy, a preference dataset shows it pairs and says: "this one is better than that one — learn the difference."

Why we need this at all

SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning — teaching by showing ideal answers to copy) is great at teaching format and behaviour. But it has a blind spot: it only ever sees good answers. It never sees a slightly-worse answer, so it has no sense of better vs worse.

Many real qualities are exactly like that — politeness, helpfulness, safety, not rambling. These are hard to write down as one perfect target, but easy for a human to judge when shown two options side by side.

Analogy one — wine tasting. To teach someone good taste in wine, you could hand them one "perfect" glass and say "drink this forever" (that is SFT). Or you could pour two glasses and ask "which do you prefer?", again and again. After hundreds of comparisons they grow a palate — a feel for what better tastes like, even on wines they have never met. Preference data builds the model's palate.

Analogy two — hiring. Describing the perfect employee in the abstract is painfully hard. But put two candidates in front of a manager and ask "who would you hire?" — they answer instantly. Comparisons are easier and more reliable than absolute scores, and preference datasets lean on exactly this human strength.

What one row looks like

The standard shape has three parts: the prompt, a chosen response (the preferred one), and a rejected response (the worse one).

{
  "prompt": "Explain photosynthesis to a 6-year-old.",
  "chosen": "Plants are like tiny chefs! They take sunlight, water, and air, and cook their own food. Cool, right?",
  "rejected": "Photosynthesis is the conversion of CO2 and H2O into glucose via chlorophyll-mediated light reactions."
}

Both answers are correct. The rejected one just misses the brief — it is not wrong, it is worse for this prompt. That subtlety is the point: preference data captures fuzzy, contextual quality that a single gold answer cannot.

Where the labels come from

Three common sources, usually mixed together:

  • Humans read both responses and click the better one — the gold standard, but slow and pricey (see Human Evals).
  • An AI judge — a strong model asked "which is better and why?" — labels pairs cheaply and fast. This is AI feedback, and it powers most modern pipelines; see Synthetic Datasets.
  • Implicit signals from real use — the answer a user copied, kept, or thumbs-upped beats the one they regenerated or down-voted.

How the data gets used

Once you have these pairs, two main recipes turn them into a better model:

  • DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) trains the model to directly raise the probability of chosen answers and lower it for rejected ones — simple, and now the default.
  • RLHF (the older route) first trains a separate reward model — a scorer that predicts which answer humans prefer — then uses it as a coach.

Both start from the exact same preference pairs; they just consume them differently.

SFT datasetPreference dataset
One ideal answer per promptTwo answers per prompt + a winner
Teaches: copy thisTeaches: prefer this over that
Good for format, tone, skillsGood for fuzzy quality, safety, helpfulness
Used by ordinary fine-tuningUsed by DPO and RLHF

A practical note: preference tuning is the polish, not the foundation. You almost always do SFT first to get the basics, then layer preference data on top to sand down the rough edges. Skipping straight to preferences on a raw model is like teaching wine palate to someone who has never tasted food — there is no baseline to refine.

💡 The single most valuable thing to vary is the prompts; the single most valuable thing to keep tight is what "better" means. Decide your definition of better before you collect a single pair, and write it down as a rubric.
mentalModel: A preference dataset is a stack of "this vs that" cards: same question, two answers, a circle around the winner. The model studies thousands of these and grows a palate — a feel for better-vs-worse it can apply to questions it has never seen.
takeaways: Each row = one prompt + a chosen answer + a rejected answer.
Teaches better-vs-worse, not just one ideal answer like SFT.
SFT first, then polish with these pairs via DPO or RLHF.

diagram.caption: Each row pairs a chosen and a rejected answer to the same prompt; DPO or RLHF learns to widen the gap between them.

mistakes: Pairs that are too far apart. If chosen is brilliant and rejected is gibberish, the model only learns "avoid garbage" — easy and useless. The valuable signal lives in close pairs where both answers are plausible and one is just slightly better.

An inconsistent definition of "better." If one labeller rewards brevity and another rewards detail, your winners contradict each other and the model learns noise. Agree on a written rubric first, then check labellers agree on it.

Skipping SFT and jumping straight to preferences. Preference tuning refines an already-decent model; it cannot build basic skills from scratch. Do SFT or Instruction Tuning first, then polish with preferences.

Reward hacking / length bias. Models often learn that longer, more confident, or more flattering answers tend to "win," then game that signal — getting wordier and more sycophantic without getting better. Balance answer lengths and reward substance, not padding.

Generating both responses from the same model at the same settings. The two answers come out nearly identical, so there is almost nothing to prefer. Create real contrast: different models, different prompts, or different temperatures.

Trusting an AI judge blindly. AI labellers are fast but carry their own biases (they often favour their own style or the first option shown). Spot-check a sample against human labels and shuffle the order of the two answers — see Data Curation.
exercise:
Build a tiny preference dataset by hand. Pick one task — say, "reply to an angry customer email." Write 8 rows in JSONL, each with a prompt, a chosen response, and a rejected response. The key rule: make every rejected answer plausible but slightly worse (a touch too formal, slightly defensive, or a little long) — never obviously broken. Write down a one-paragraph rubric defining what "better" means for this task before you start, and judge every pair against it.

Stretch goal: Take 3 of your prompts, generate both responses from a real model (one at low temperature, one at high), label which you prefer, and note whether your earlier rubric still held up or needed sharpening once you saw real model output.
solution: Hint: A clean row looks like {"prompt":"...","chosen":"...","rejected":"..."}, one object per line, no trailing commas. Good rejected answers share most of the chosen answer's content but lose on one dimension from your rubric — that keeps the pair "close" so the model learns a real distinction, not just "avoid nonsense." Validate with python -c "import json; [json.loads(l) for l in open('prefs.jsonl')]". On the stretch goal: most people find their rubric was too vague ("be empathetic") and needed concrete tells ("acknowledge the problem in the first sentence; offer one specific next step"). That sharpened rubric is the real product — the pairs are just it written out as data.
summary: A preference dataset is a set of prompt + chosen + rejected triples: same question, two answers, a label saying which won. It teaches a model better-vs-worse for fuzzy qualities (helpfulness, tone, safety) that a single ideal answer can't capture. Labels come from humans, AI judges, or real-usage signals. You SFT first, then polish with these pairs via DPO or RLHF — and the value lives in close, consistently-judged pairs.

keyTerms.def: A collection of rows where each row has one prompt and two responses, plus a label saying which response is preferred.
The two responses in a preference pair: chosen is the preferred (better) one, rejected is the worse one for that prompt.
Judging two options against each other ("which is better?") instead of scoring each one alone. Easier and more reliable for humans.
A separate model trained on preference pairs to predict which answer humans would prefer; used as the scorer in RLHF.
Using a strong model as the judge to label which response is better, instead of (or alongside) human labellers. Fast and cheap.
When a model games the preference signal — e.g. getting longer or more flattering — to 'win' without actually getting better.
The common tendency for longer answers to be marked as 'better,' which can quietly train models to ramble.
A written definition of what 'better' means for your task, agreed before labelling so different judges stay consistent.
Preference signals collected from real use — copies, keeps, thumbs-ups, or regenerations — rather than from explicit labelling.

quiz.q: What does a single row in a preference dataset contain?
options: One prompt and one ideal answer to copy
One prompt, a chosen response, and a rejected response
A list of correct answers ranked by length
A prompt and a numeric quality score from 1 to 10
quiz.explain: A preference row is a contest: one prompt with two responses, labelled chosen (better) and rejected (worse). A single ideal answer is what SFT uses instead.

quiz.q: Why is a preference dataset useful when SFT already exists?
options: It is cheaper to collect than any other dataset type
It removes the need to do SFT at all
It teaches better-vs-worse for fuzzy qualities a single ideal answer can't capture
It makes the model run faster at inference time
quiz.explain: SFT only ever shows good answers, so it never learns what slightly-worse looks like. Preference pairs teach contextual quality like tone, helpfulness, and safety.

quiz.q: You build pairs where every chosen answer is excellent and every rejected answer is total gibberish. What goes wrong?
options: The model only learns to 'avoid garbage' and gains little useful signal
Nothing — the bigger the gap, the better the model learns
DPO will refuse to train on it
quiz.explain: The valuable signal lives in close pairs where both answers are plausible and one is just slightly better. Far-apart pairs teach an easy, near-useless distinction.

quiz.q: A team trains a model purely on preference pairs, skipping SFT, then complains it can't follow basic formats. What's the likely cause?
options: Their AI judge was biased toward the first option
Preference tuning polishes an already-capable model; it can't build basic skills from scratch
They used DPO instead of RLHF
Their chosen and rejected answers were too close together
quiz.explain: Preference tuning is the polish, not the foundation. You do SFT or instruction tuning first to install basic skills, then layer preferences on top.
